{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"call_a36dc9779fbc48a48288a0e7","type":"tool_result","content":"1\t# aide-de-camp: Application Plan\n2\t\n3\t## Overview\n4\t\n5\taide-de-camp is a universal personal interface that accepts stream-of-consciousness voice and text input, routes it to parallel agents across any domain, and renders results as a live canvas of HTML cards or as synthesized audio narration. It is built on top of existing personal infrastructure and improves itself through conversational feedback after initial bootstrap.\n6\t\n7\tCLI invocation: `adc`\n8\t\n9\t---\n10\t\n11\t## Problem Statement\n12\t\n13\tManaging multiple active projects requires constant context-switching between dedicated tabs, terminals, and chat sessions. The input channel (typing into a specific Claude Code tab) is also the routing layer, and that conflation adds cognitive overhead. The same problem extends beyond coding: research, lookups, personal context, reminders — anything that requires knowing which system to ask before you can ask it.\n14\t\n15\taide-de-camp eliminates that overhead by providing a single input surface that routes automatically, dispatches in parallel, and returns organized results without requiring the user to know where to ask.\n16\t\n17\t---\n18\t\n19\t## Core Architecture\n20\t\n21\t### The Hot Path\n22\t\n23\t```\n24\tUser utterance (voice or text)\n25\t  → STT transcription (Web Speech API or whisper-stt)\n26\t  → Intent Router (one LLM call, haiku-class, ~500ms est.)\n27\t      output: [{project_slug, intent_type, urgency, utterance_fragment,\n28\t                lookup_kind (lookup intents only)}, ...]\n29\t  → N parallel Fetch+Synthesize workers (one per intent thread)\n30\t      Fetch: deterministic code, executes command matrix based on intent_type\n31\t             (lookup intents: intent_type + lookup_kind)\n32\t      Synthesize: one LLM call per thread (sonnet-class, ~1-2s est.)\n33\t      output: {data, summary} — urgency carries through unchanged from the\n34\t              intent thread (see Urgency ownership)\n35\t  → Server-side card render (no LLM): deterministic component lookup via\n36\t      component_usage_patterns, template filled into card_cache;\n37\t      no match → result flagged for the built-in fallback card\n38\t  → Cards streamed via SSE to active canvas — client injects the rendered\n39\t      HTML, or renders fallback-flagged results with its built-in generic card\n40\t```\n41\t\n42\tTarget: < 3 seconds from utterance to first partial result on canvas. No Claude Code session startup on this path.\n43\t\n44\t> The ~500ms and ~1-2s figures above — and every per-stage number in this plan — are design **estimates**. Nothing on the hot path has been measured yet. See Latency Budget & Instrumentation.\n45\t\n46\t### Latency Budget & Instrumentation\n47\t\n48\tThe <3s promise is the product's central claim, so it gets a budget, instrumentation, and a gate — not just a diagram annotation. Per-stage targets below are estimates chosen so the end-to-end budget is plausible; the Measured columns show actual captured timings from 206 production-like runs across three test shapes (see bead adc-2xf52 for detailed analysis).\n49\t\n50\t| Stage | Target (ESTIMATE) | Measured p50 | Measured p95 |\n51\t|-------|-------------------|--------------|--------------|\n52\t| STT final transcript (Web Speech API) | ~300ms | *Not measured* (client-side) | *Not measured* (client-side) |\n53\t| Intent Router (haiku-class via ZAI proxy) | ~500ms | **2,808ms** ❌ (Jul 24) | **5,558ms** ❌ (Jul 24) |\n54\t| Fetch — first source returns (surfaces as a per-source progress state on the pending card) | ~500ms | **0-16ms** ✅ | **0-21ms** ✅ |\n55\t| Fetch — window closes (all sources resolved or timed out; gates synthesize start — see Fetch Strand) | ~1s | **0-51ms** ✅ | **0-191ms** ✅ |\n56\t| Synthesize — first token (sonnet-class via ZAI proxy; starts at fetch-window close) | ~1s (cap set by the e2e gate — see internal-consistency note) | *Not measured* (instrumentation gap) | *Not measured* (instrumentation gap) |\n57\t| Synthesize — total (first token through completion) | ~1-2s | **3,108-3,984ms** ❌ | **4,663-7,877ms** ❌ |\n58\t| SSE emit → first card render | ~100ms | *Not measured* | *Not measured* |\n59\t| Escalate — bead formulation + safety validation + `bf create` (haiku-class via ZAI proxy; off the first-card path, see note below) | ~2s | **3,992ms** ❌ | **5,445ms** ❌ |\n60\t| **End-to-end: utterance end → first partial card** | **< 3s** | **5,219-5,571ms** ❌ | **8,853-10,404ms** ❌ |\n61\t\n62\t**Internal consistency — the e2e row is the binding gate.** The hot-path stages are strictly sequential (STT → router → fetch-window close → synthesize first token → SSE emit), so their targets must sum inside the end-to-end budget: 300 + 500 + 1000 + 1000 + 100 ms ≈ 2.9s against the 3s gate, leaving ~100ms of unallocated slack. (The fetch first-source and escalate rows sit off this critical path — the former lands inside the fetch window, the latter is off the first-card path per the note below.) Synthesize-first-token was originally sketched at ~1–2s — the Hot Path diagram's ~1–2s figure survives as the estimate for the *full* synthesize call, first token through completion — but at a 2s first token the stages sum to ~3.9s: every stage could pass its own budget individually while the end-to-end gate fails, a contradiction that would otherwise surface only at rehearsal. Hence the ~1s first-token allocation above. All per-stage targets remain ESTIMATES and are **provisional allocations, not independent pass/fail gates**: once the Measured p50/p95 columns fill, re-derive the stage allocations from measured slack (a stage running under budget donates headroom to one running over) with the e2e row held fixed and overriding — the Gate below then applies against the re-derived allocations. A run where every stage meets its allocation but the e2e row misses is still a failed run.\n63\t\n64\t**Task-profile first card.** The escalate stage does not gate the <3s promise: for a task-profile thread, the ack/pending card (a served built-in template, not a component-library render — see Component Library → Built-in cards) renders as soon as the router resolves the thread (router + SSE emit only) — before bead formulation and `bf create` complete — then updates in place with the bead ref when the escalate stage returns. The escalate row above budgets that in-place update, not the first card; the end-to-end row applies to the ack card.\n65\t\n66\t**Instrumentation requirement.** The server records per-stage timings for every dispatch — `router_ms`, `fetch_first_source_ms`, `fetch_total_ms` (fetch-window close), `synthesize_first_token_ms`, `synthesize_total_ms`, `escalate_ms` (task-profile dispatches: formulation + validation + `bf create`), `sse_emit_ms`, plus client-reported STT and first-render timestamps when available — and persists them to the session store's `dispatch_timings` table (schema in Data Model). This is not optional telemetry: it is the only way the Measured columns get filled, and it doubles as the per-step timing log the Phase 5 rehearsal checklist requires.\n67\t\n68\t**Gate.** ❌ **DEMO BLOCKED** — Latency budget compliance **FAILED**. The Measured p50/p95 columns have been filled from real runs, and the system does **NOT** meet acceptable performance targets for demo purposes.\n69\t\n70\t**Gate Status (2026-07-24):** Budget compliance verification (adc-1jrkq) shows router latency at 2,651ms p50 (5.3× over 500ms budget) and 6,441ms p95 (4.3× over 1,500ms budget). End-to-end latency exceeds the 3s promise by 2-2.5×. Per the plan's explicit gate criteria, the demo cannot proceed until either: (1) router latency is reduced to meet budget targets, or (2) the on-screen promise is changed to reflect actual performance.\n71\t\n72\t### The Async Path\n73\t\n74\t```\n75\tTask-profile intent (research, coding, long-running work)\n76\t  → Generated-Bead Safety validation + approval (see Escalate Strand)\n77\t  → NEEDLE task bead created (bf create)\n78\t  → Existing Claude Code workers pick up bead normally\n79\t  → Bead watcher detects closure event\n80\t  → Result written to session store results table\n81\t  → SSE push to active canvas (or Telegram if no canvas)\n82\t```\n83\t\n84\t> ⚠ The \"or Telegram if no canvas\" leg is NON-FUNCTIONAL today — Telegram delivery has never worked in any deployment (stubbed methods, unreachable bridge URL; see ADR-1). Until ADR-1 lands, a result arriving with no active canvas is persisted to the session store but reaches no surface.\n85\t\n86\tThe diagram above is the happy path only. Beads also get refused, fail repeatedly, or sit unclaimed — and NEEDLE has no built-in circuit breaker: it re-dispatches a failing bead forever. The async path therefore also specifies:\n87\t\n88\t- **Re-dispatch circuit breaker.** NEEDLE exposes no dispatch-attempt API, so the breaker's signal is the bead's own comment stream: a worker that declines a bead appends a structured refusal comment — `bf comment <id> \"REFUSED: <reason>\"` — the same convention the live incident's workers followed when they committed refusal records to the bead. Each tick the watcher checks its open tracked beads (every unresolved `intents.bead_ref`) alongside the closed list, parses comments past a per-bead high-water mark, and persists the running refusal count in the watcher-owned `bead_watch` table (see Data Model) — on disk, so breaker state survives watcher restarts. After N refusals (default 3) or T hours without progress (default 24h — no closure and no new non-refusal comment), it fences the bead — `bf update` to `status=blocked`, so workers stop picking it up — sets the intent's status to `stuck`, and pushes a \"task stuck — needs your input\" card to the active surface with the most recent refusal reason attached.\n89\t- **Terminal failures.** A bead closed as failed/refused resolves its intent to `failed`; the reason surfaces as a card, never silently dropped. User cancellation still maps to `cancelled`.\n90\t- **Visible aging.** Pending cards show elapsed time; a card past its SLA is flagged on canvas before the breaker trips, so a stuck task is never invisible. The SLA is defined, not vibes: built-in per-intent-type defaults (task-profile: 6h; hot-path intents flag at 30s pending, since their budget is 3s) with a per-project `sla_hours` override in the registry entry. Flag ownership is split by path: for bead-backed (task-profile) intents the watcher computes `sla_deadline` at bead creation, stores it in `bead_watch`, and stamps `sla_flagged_at` when the flag fires. Hot-path intents have no bead and no watcher row — their 30s flag is owned by the **canvas client**: a pure client-side timer from the local pending placeholder's creation at submit time (the canvas creates the placeholder before any server response — see Escalate Strand, Pending/ack card render path) applies the aged-pending treatment with no server round-trip, deliberately, because a hung or wedged server is exactly the failure this flag must survive (see the matching Degraded-State UX row).\n91\t\n92\t### The Self-Improvement Path\n93\t\n94\t```\n95\tFeedback signal (explicit instruction or implicit engagement pattern)\n96\t  → Self-modification agent (Claude Code via NEEDLE task bead)\n97\t      reads target artifact (prompt file, registry YAML)\n98\t      generates update\n99\t      surfaces diff to user\n100\t  → User approves → artifact written → hot-reloaded on next invocation\n101\t```\n102\t\n103\tComponent templates are deliberately absent from the artifact list above: visual/component feedback follows the same feedback → diff → approval loop, but routes through the UI-regen agent, the sole writer of component *definitions* in the library — the `components`, `component_versions`, and `component_tags` tables in `data/components.db`. The hot-path server also writes to that DB on every dispatch, but only mechanical render/usage state (`card_cache` rows and the usage-stat columns), never definitions. One flow, two agents, disjoint write scopes — see Security Model.\n104\t\n105\t---\n106\t\n107\t## Components\n108\t\n109\t### 1. Intent Router\n110\t\n111\t**Runtime:** Direct API call (haiku-class via ZAI proxy)\n112\t\n113\tOne LLM call per utterance. Receives the full utterance text, the project registry, and a segmentation prompt. Returns a JSON array of intent threads, each tagged with:\n114\t\n115\t```json\n116\t[\n117\t  {\n118\t    \"project_slug\": \"pbx-web\",\n119\t    \"intent_type\": \"status\",\n120\t    \"urgency\": \"normal\",\n121\t    \"utterance_fragment\": \"has the pbx web caught up?\"\n122\t  },\n123\t  {\n124\t    \"project_slug\": \"whisper-stt\",\n125\t    \"intent_type\": \"status\",\n126\t    \"urgency\": \"normal\",\n127\t    \"utterance_fragment\": \"what's the state of whisper stt\"\n128\t  },\n129\t  {\n130\t    \"project_slug\": \"whisper-stt\",\n131\t    \"intent_type\": \"lookup\",\n132\t    \"lookup_kind\": \"logs\",\n133\t    \"urgency\": \"normal\",\n134\t    \"utterance_fragment\": \"and pull up its recent logs\"\n135\t  }\n136\t]\n137\t```\n138\t\n139\t`lookup_kind` appears only on `lookup` threads (see the intent-type list below); no other intent type carries it.\n140\t\n141\tIntent types: `status`, `action`, `brainstorm`, `lookup`, `reminder`, `self-modification`, `monitoring-config`, `task-profile`, `clarification`\n142\t\n143\t- **status**: Query current state (pods, pipelines, deployments, beads)\n144\t- **action**: Execute a command (deploy, restart, create) — executes only through the Action Execution Model (declarative-config Git operations + ArgoCD sync status, or reviewed escalation beads); never direct kubectl mutation\n145\t- **brainstorm**: Explore options, design, architecture discussion\n146\t- **lookup**: Find specific information. Every lookup thread carries a router-emitted `lookup_kind` — `logs` | `config` | `docs` (default `docs` when the utterance names nothing sharper) — because \"recent logs\" and \"deployment config\" for the same project are different fetches and different cards, not one. `lookup_kind` selects the fetch matrix (`prompts/fetch/lookup-{lookup_kind}.md`) and is embedded in the result's `result_type` (`lookup:{lookup_kind}:{project_slug}`, e.g. `lookup:logs:whisper-stt` vs `lookup:config:whisper-stt`), so log-lookup and config-lookup results select components independently. Persisted on the intent row (`intents.lookup_kind`)\n147\t- **reminder**: Set or query reminders — **NOT YET IMPLEMENTED**: no reminders table, scheduler, or module exists. The router does not dispatch this type; a reminder-shaped utterance is handled as `clarification` with a \"reminders aren't available yet\" card. Minimal design sketch in Future Work\n148\t- **self-modification**: Instructions to improve the interface itself\n149\t- **monitoring-config**: Configure ambient monitoring rules\n150\t- **task-profile**: Durable async work items that escalate to NEEDLE beads\n151\t- **clarification**: Low-confidence routing outcome requiring user input (meta-type, not dispatched)\n152\t\n153\tThe router reloads its segmentation prompt and the project registry per call through an mtime-checked cache: each file is stat'd on every call and re-read only when its mtime has changed — the same strategy every hot-reloaded artifact uses (see Hot-Reload Architecture). Hot-reload is automatic; an edit takes effect on the next call.\n154\t\n155\t**Urgency ownership:** the router assigns the initial `urgency` on every intent thread as part of hot-path segmentation, guided by `prompts/urgency.md` (folded into the single router call — no extra LLM call on the hot path). The escalate strand refines urgency when formulating task-profile beads, and rule-driven ambient-monitoring results — which have no intent thread behind them — take their urgency from the firing rule in `config/monitoring.yaml` (see Bead Watcher). No other component assigns urgency; in particular, synthesize never does — it outputs `{data, summary}` and the thread's router-assigned urgency carries through to the result unchanged.\n156\t\n157\tAmbiguous intents: if confidence is below threshold, router returns an `intent_type: \"clarification\"` thread. The voice model or canvas handles the clarification round-trip before dispatching.\n158\t\n159\t### 2. Project Registry\n160\t\n161\t**Format:** YAML file, read per-invocation by the router\n162\t\n163\tDefines projects the router knows about:\n164\t\n165\t```yaml\n166\tprojects:\n167\t  pbx-web:\n168\t    aliases: [\"pbx\", \"the pbx\", \"phone system\"]\n169\t    description: \"PBX web interface on ardenone-cluster\"\n170\t    cluster: ardenone-cluster  # ArgoCD endpoint for this cluster resolves via config/clusters.yaml (see Fetch Strand — Cluster→ArgoCD Endpoint Resolution)\n171\t    namespace: pbx-web\n172\t    repo_path: /home/coding/declarative-config  # local checkout: git-log + per-project bead listing (pbx-web manifests live in k8s/ardenone-cluster/pbx-web/)\n173\t    argocd_app: pbx-web  # ArgoCD Application name for the fetch matrix's sync row; defaults to the project slug when omitted\n174\t    sla_hours: 6          # pending-card SLA override (see The Async Path — Visible aging)\n175\t    intent_support: [status, action, brainstorm, lookup, task-profile]\n176\t    # `action` appears in intent_support for routing completeness, but action\n177\t    # dispatch is DISABLED until the Action strand ships — see Action Execution\n178\t    # Model (Status: executor NOT BUILT)\n179\t    workflows:\n180\t      deploy:\n181\t        # DESIGN-ONLY — no executor interprets `steps` yet; the step vocabulary\n182\t        # is defined in Action Execution Model → \"Future: Action strand\".\n183\t        # Mutating steps follow the Action Execution Model: gitops-commit\n184\t        # edits jedarden/declarative-config; argocd-sync-status polls the\n185\t        # read-only ArgoCD API until the app reports Synced/Healthy\n186\t        steps: [ci-status, image-tag, gitops-commit, argocd-sync-status, pod-status]\n187\t\n188\t  whisper-stt:\n189\t    aliases: [\"whisper\", \"stt\", \"speech-to-text\"]\n190\t    description: \"Whisper STT service on ardenone-cluster\"\n191\t    cluster: ardenone-cluster\n192\t    namespace: whisper-stt\n193\t    repo_path: /home/coding/declarative-config  # local checkout: git-log + per-project bead listing (whisper-stt manifests live in k8s/ardenone-cluster/whisper-stt/)\n194\t    argocd_app: whisper-stt  # explicit here for clarity; equals the slug default\n195\t    intent_support: [status, brainstorm, lookup, task-profile]\n196\t```\n197\t\n198\tUpdated by the self-modification agent. `src/environment/discovery.py` (the repo scanner) only *seeds* the registry — an initial or explicitly re-run scan that proposes entries; after seeding, the self-modification agent is the sole ongoing author, and the scanner never overwrites an entry the agent has written. Hot-reloaded on every router call.\n199\t\n200\t### 3. Fetch Strand\n201\t\n202\t**Runtime:** Deterministic code, no LLM\n203\t\n204\tExecutes a command matrix based on `intent_type` and the project's registry entry — for `lookup` intents, on `intent_type` **plus** the router-emitted `lookup_kind`, so a logs lookup and a config lookup against the same project run different matrices. No LLM decisions — the commands are determined by the intent type (and lookup kind) and project config.\n205\t\n206\tThe command matrix itself lives in `prompts/fetch/{intent_type}.md` — except `lookup`, which has one matrix per kind: `prompts/fetch/lookup-{lookup_kind}.md` (`lookup-logs.md`, `lookup-config.md`, `lookup-docs.md`). Despite the directory name, these files are **not LLM prompts** — they are declarative configuration: markdown tables with frontmatter that `src/fetch/commands.py` parses on each invocation (the hot-reload table's \"context fetch strategy\" row refers to this parse, not a model call). They are self-modifiable and git-versioned like every other artifact. The `prompts/` location is historical; a `config/fetch/` rename would be cosmetic cleanup, not a behavior change.\n207\t\n208\tExample command matrix for `intent_type: status`:\n209\t\n210\t| Source | Command |\n211\t|--------|---------|\n212\t| Pod status | `kubectl get pods -n {namespace}` |\n213\t| ArgoCD sync | `curl {argocd_api}/api/v1/applications/{argocd_app}` — `argocd_app` from the registry entry (defaults to the project slug when omitted); `{argocd_api}` resolved from the entry's `cluster` via `config/clusters.yaml` (see Cluster→ArgoCD Endpoint Resolution below) |\n214\t| Git log | `git -C {repo_path} log -10 --oneline` |\n215\t| Bead list | `bf list --status open` — run in the project's repo checkout when it has one, with **no** `--project` filter (a project's own workspace doesn't tag its beads with its aide-de-camp slug; filtering there returns zero rows). The `--project {slug}` filter applies only on the no-checkout fallback: `bf list --project {slug} --status open` in the aide-de-camp workspace (see Beads-Workspace Scoping) |\n216\t| CI status | `kubectl get workflows -n argo-workflows -l project={slug}` |\n217\t\n218\tResults are structured data passed to the Synthesize strand. Fetch runs each source concurrently under per-source timeouts (declared alongside each command in the fetch config); the fetch window closes when every source has resolved or timed out, and only then does Synthesize fire — once, on the full set. Sources that finish early surface immediately as lightweight per-source progress states on the pending card (\"3/5 sources in\"), which is how fetch progress streams to canvas; sources that miss the window are never re-synthesized — they appear only as `fetch_coverage` caveats.\n219\t\n220\tA `fetch_coverage` field tracks which sources succeeded and which failed. Failed sources are surfaced as caveats in the result.\n221\t\n222\t#### Cluster→ArgoCD Endpoint Resolution\n223\t\n224\tThere is no single ArgoCD API. Applications on ardenone-cluster live on ardenone-manager's ArgoCD, which exposes a no-auth read-only proxy; apexalgo-iad and the other iad-* Spot clusters are managed by the ArgoCD instance on **rs-manager**, which today has **no equivalent no-auth read-only proxy** — only the authenticated, Tailscale-only API at `argocd-rs-manager.tail1b1987.ts.net:8080`. Querying the wrong instance returns not-found — indistinguishable from \"app doesn't exist\" — and puts a `fetch_coverage` caveat strip on the card. The fetch strand therefore resolves the ArgoCD endpoint from the registry entry's `cluster` through `config/clusters.yaml` (hot-reloaded via the same mtime-checked cache as every config artifact):\n225\t\n226\t```yaml\n227\t# config/clusters.yaml — cluster → ArgoCD API mapping\n228\tclusters:\n229\t  ardenone-cluster:\n230\t    argocd_api: https://argocd-ro-ardenone-manager-ts.ardenone.com:8444  # ArgoCD on ardenone-manager\n231\t    access: read-only-proxy   # no auth; the proxy injects a read-only token\n232\t  apexalgo-iad:\n233\t    argocd_api: https://argocd-rs-manager.tail1b1987.ts.net:8080         # ArgoCD on rs-manager\n234\t    access: authenticated     # ⚠ no no-auth RO proxy exists for rs-manager today — see known-issues register\n235\t  # iad-options, iad-kalshi, and other iad-* Spot clusters also map to rs-manager's ArgoCD\n236\t```\n237\t\n238\tA cluster absent from this file, or mapped with an `access` mode the fetch strand cannot satisfy (it holds no ArgoCD credentials, so it can only consume `read-only-proxy` endpoints), resolves the ArgoCD source as **failed** — an honest `fetch_coverage` caveat, never a silent wrong-instance query. **Demo consequence:** both scripted projects declare `cluster: apexalgo-iad`, so their ArgoCD state is readable only from rs-manager — a must-fix-before-demo row in the Phase 5 known-issues register: either provision an rs-manager read-only proxy mirroring the ardenone-manager pattern (and flip its `clusters.yaml` entry to `read-only-proxy`), or every scripted status card carries a caveat strip and criterion 3 fails the take.\n239\t\n240\t### 4. Synthesize Strand\n241\t\n242\t**Runtime:** Direct API call (sonnet-class via ZAI proxy)\n243\t\n244\tTakes `{intent_spec, fetched_context}` → produces `{data, summary}`.\n245\t\n246\t- `data`: structured JSON matching the component library's expected schema for this intent type\n247\t- `summary`: 1-3 sentence narration suitable for audio mode\n248\t\n249\tSingle-turn inference, invoked exactly once per thread when the fetch window closes (all sources resolved or timed out — see Fetch Strand); a source arriving after the window never triggers re-synthesis, it shows up only as a `fetch_coverage` caveat. The call streams its output tokens so the card fills progressively — output streaming, not incremental fetch input, is what puts a partial card on canvas early. Reads its system prompt from a file per invocation (hot-reload). The synthesize prompt is the highest-leverage artifact in the system — it defines result format, detail level, and what fields to include.\n250\t\n251\tRuns once per intent thread in parallel across all active threads.\n252\t\n253\t### 5. Escalate Strand\n254\t\n255\t**Runtime:** Direct API call + `bf create`\n256\t\n257\tFor task-profile intents that need durable async handling:\n258\t1. One LLM call to formulate a bead body from the intent + context, refining the router-assigned urgency for the bead\n259\t2. The body passes Generated-Bead Safety validation (below); action-derived beads additionally wait for user approval\n260\t3. `bf create` in the aide-de-camp beads workspace, tagged `--project {slug}` (see Beads-Workspace Scoping below)\n261\t4. Returns a pending-card spec with the bead reference\n262\t\n263\t**Pending/ack card render path.** The pending/ack card is **not** a component-library render: no `results` row exists until bead closure, so there is nothing for `results.result_type` selection to key on and no `result_id` for `card_cache`. Pending/ack cards — including per-source fetch-progress and elapsed-time states — are built-in frontend templates shipped in `src/canvas/`, exactly like the welcome and generic fallback cards (see Component Library → Built-in cards). The pending lifecycle starts client-side, not with a server event: at submit time the canvas creates a **local per-utterance pending placeholder** — before any server response has arrived, so even a hung server leaves a card on canvas to age — which splits into per-thread pending cards when the dispatch ack arrives. The client fills those from SSE events (dispatch ack, per-source progress, bead ref on `bf create` return) and replaces them with the component-rendered card when the real result lands; the 30s aged-pending timer runs from the local placeholder's creation, not from any SSE event (see the Degraded-State UX aged-pending row).\n264\t\n265\tThe bead watcher subsequently bridges bead closure to result delivery.\n266\t\n267\t#### Beads-Workspace Scoping\n268\t\n269\t\"The appropriate project\" is deliberately **not** another repo's `.beads/`. Every aide-de-camp-originated bead lives in the aide-de-camp repo's own beads workspace, carrying its target project as the `--project {slug}` field. One workspace for create, poll, and fence means the escalate strand and the bead watcher run every `bf` invocation from the aide-de-camp checkout directory and are guaranteed to see every bead they own; NEEDLE workers roam across workspaces and pick these up like any other.\n270\t\n271\tThe one exception is read-only: the fetch matrix's per-project bead listing reports on a project's *own* backlog, so it runs `bf list --status open` in that project's repo checkout when the registry entry has a `repo_path` with a `.beads/` workspace — deliberately with **no** `--project` filter: a project's own workspace doesn't tag its beads with its aide-de-camp slug, so `--project {slug}` there would return zero or near-zero rows and render a plausible-looking empty backlog on camera. The `--project {slug}` filter belongs only to the fallback: when there is no local workspace, fetch falls back to the aide-de-camp workspace filtered by `--project {slug}` — still real data, but only aide-de-camp-originated beads — and `fetch_coverage` records the narrower scope so the card carries a caveat (\"no local beads workspace for {slug}; showing aide-de-camp-originated beads only\").\n272\t\n273\t#### Generated-Bead Safety\n274\t\n275\tAn LLM-authored bead body is an instruction that autonomous workers will execute. Unconstrained, this is the system's sharpest edge — live evidence: an escalate-authored bead containing an unscoped `kubectl delete pod` instruction put NEEDLE workers into a refusal loop (workers correctly refuse it; NEEDLE re-dispatches it forever). Every escalate-authored bead body therefore passes a deterministic validation gate before `bf create`:\n276\t\n277\t- **Cluster-mutation verbs are denied.** Bead bodies must not instruct workers to run `kubectl apply/create/delete/scale/patch/edit/annotate/rollout` or any other live mutation of a managed resource. These violate the GitOps rule and workers refuse them.\n278\t- **Mutations must be phrased as declarative-config edits.** Any desired change to cluster state is written as \"edit the manifest under `k8s/<cluster>/...` in `jedarden/declarative-config`, commit, push; ArgoCD syncs\" — the only sanctioned mutation path (see Action Execution Model).\n279\t- **Scoping is required.** Bead bodies that reference cluster resources must name the cluster, namespace, and specific resource. Unscoped operational instructions (\"delete the pod\", \"restart everything\") are rejected.\n280\t\n281\tValidation failure → the bead is not created; the escalate strand re-formulates once with the failure reason in context, and surfaces a clarification card if the second attempt also fails.\n282\t\n283\t**User-approval gate:** no action-derived bead (anything instructing workers to change state — deploys, config edits, restarts, deletions) is created without explicit approval. The proposed bead body is rendered as a card on canvas (narrated in audio mode); approve → `bf create`, reject → discard. Purely informational task-profile beads (research, lookups) skip the approval gate but never the validation pass.\n284\t\n285\t### 6. Voice Model\n286\t\n287\t**Runtime:** OpenAI Realtime API (persistent session, via the DUCK-E session handler — see Technology Stack)\n288\t\n289\tThe conversational layer. Receives transcribed utterances (or audio directly), calls `dispatch_intent()` tool to trigger routing, and narrates results at appropriate moments.\n290\t\n291\tTool calls are triggers, not queries. `dispatch_intent()` returns an acknowledgment immediately; results arrive out-of-band and are surfaced by the voice model at natural conversational pauses.\n292\t\n293\tThe voice model's system prompt controls narration style, batching behavior, urgency handling, and multi-turn topic tracking. Read per session-turn from a file (hot-reload).\n294\t\n295\tIn audio mode there is no canvas. The voice model reads `result.summary` directly. When a user transitions from audio to canvas, pending results are rendered using the component library and appear on canvas — session continuity means the canvas catches up.\n296\t\n297\t### 7. UI-Regen Agent\n298\t\n299\t**Runtime:** Claude Code via NEEDLE (task bead)\n300\t\n301\tSteward of the component library — asynchronous only. It is never on the hot path and never renders a card a user is waiting on.\n302\t\n303\t**Hot-path selection and rendering happen in the server, not here.** On every dispatch the server picks the component deterministically — highest `match_score` in `component_usage_patterns` for the result's `result_type`, no LLM call (lookup result_types embed the router's `lookup_kind`, so `lookup:logs:whisper-stt` and `lookup:config:whisper-stt` are distinct keys selecting distinct components) — fills its template with the result data per layout bucket, writes the output to `card_cache`, and streams the rendered HTML over SSE; the client injects it. When nothing matches (a first-ever result shape, or no score above threshold), the server flags the result and the client renders it with the built-in generic fallback card (see Component Library) — a novel shape never blanks the canvas, it just renders plainly until the library catches up.\n304\t\n305\t**Escaping contract (render path).** Template fill is the escaping boundary: every interpolated value — fetch output, `result.data` fields, `summary`, raw log lines — is HTML-escaped at template-fill time, and templates receive data as text only, never raw HTML from results, so a markup-looking log line renders as literal text instead of breaking layout or executing in the canvas. This binds LLM-generated component templates and the built-in generic fallback card's key/value grid alike (the fallback escapes client-side, where it renders). The same contract binds every client-filled built-in card: SSE-event values interpolated into built-in templates on the client — per-source progress states, bead refs, elapsed-time strings, error details, and above all worker/LLM-authored free text like the stuck card's refusal reasons lifted from bead comments — are inserted as text nodes (or escaped identically to the fallback card's values), never as HTML.\n306\t\n307\tThe UI-regen agent's job is making the library catch up. Given a result shape that fell to the fallback card or a weak match:\n308\t1. Finds the best-fit existing component (semantic match against component descriptions) and records the mapping in `component_usage_patterns` so the hot-path lookup hits it next time\n309\t2. If no good fit, generates a new component from scratch and stores it\n310\t3. On feedback, iterates the component (updates library, triggers canvas re-render)\n311\t\n312\tMulti-step: reads library, generates or selects, writes back. Claude Code's file access and tool use make this the right runtime.\n313\t\n314\tCreated as a task bead; not on the hot path. Canvas updates in place when a component is versioned (SSE push: `component_updated: {component_id, version}`).\n315\t\n316\t### 8. Self-Modification Agent\n317\t\n318\t**Runtime:** Claude Code via NEEDLE (task bead)\n319\t\n320\tReads and writes the artifacts that encode system behavior:\n321\t- Router prompt (`prompts/router.md`)\n322\t- Project registry (`config/registry.yaml`)\n323\t- Strand prompts (`prompts/fetch/*.md` — one per intent type, per `lookup_kind` for lookup; `prompts/synthesize.md`; `prompts/escalate/task-profile.md`)\n324\t- Urgency classifier prompt (`prompts/urgency.md`)\n325\t- Voice model prompt (`prompts/voice.md`)\n326\t- Monitoring config (`config/monitoring.yaml`)\n327\t- Exception routing rules (`config/exceptions.yaml`)\n328\t\n329\tWorkflow for every modification:\n330\t1. Read current artifact\n331\t2. Understand intent of change\n332\t3. Generate updated artifact\n333\t4. Surface diff to user (in canvas or narrated in audio mode)\n334\t5. On approval: write artifact, hot-reload takes effect on next invocation\n335\t6. On rejection: discard\n336\t\n337\tSafety model:\n338\t- Diffs before every application — no silent changes\n339\t- Versioning is git: every artifact write (`prompts/*.md`, `config/*.yaml`) is a git commit with a machine-generated message; rolling back any update is one instruction → `git revert` of that commit. Component library artifacts get the same guarantee through the `component_versions` table. No bespoke version store.\n340\t- Confidence threshold: unambiguous changes (adding an alias) can auto-apply with notification; structural changes always require explicit approval\n341\t- Out-of-band kill switch: self-modification can break the very interface used to undo it (a bad router prompt means no more routed instructions). Two escape hatches bypass the interface entirely: `ADC_SELFMOD_FREEZE=1` in the server environment, or `adc freeze` from the CLI (which falls back to touching a `data/FREEZE` sentinel file if the server is unresponsive). While frozen, every self-modification and auto-apply write is refused, and `adc restore-artifacts` git-reverts `prompts/` and `config/` to the last-known-good commit — no canvas, voice session, or router involvement required. **Break-glass caveat:** the sentinel-file fallback and the git revert are local filesystem/git operations against the server's artifact store — `adc` is a thin HTTP client (see adc CLI connectivity), so from a remote machine neither works exactly when the server is unresponsive. The break-glass procedure is: SSH into the server host first, then run `adc freeze` / `adc restore-artifacts` there.\n342\t\n343\t### 9. Background Analysis Bead\n344\t\n345\t**Runtime:** Claude Code via NEEDLE (task bead, low priority)\n346\t\n347\tRuns on a schedule. Reads intent history and engagement signals from the session store, identifies patterns, proposes artifact updates.\n348\t\n349\tExamples:\n350\t- \"User consistently drills into status results for more detail → propose adding causality fields to synthesize prompt\"\n351\t- \"User ignores pipeline monitoring pushes for 5 days → propose lowering urgency tier\"\n352\t- \"After deploy status, user always asks about pod logs within 2 minutes → propose speculative pre-fetch\"\n353\t\n354\tProposals are surfaced as cards on canvas for user review. Auto-apply only above a high confidence threshold — and an auto-apply is not a third artifact writer: the accepted proposal executes through the self-modification agent's write path, inheriting its write scope (`prompts/` and `config/` only), its git-commit-per-write rollback guarantee (one-instruction revert), and the out-of-band kill switch — all auto-apply is refused while the freeze is engaged.\n355\t\n356\t### 10. Bead Watcher\n357\t\n358\t**Runtime:** In-process daemon loop — an asyncio background task spawned by FastAPI lifespan startup, inside the same process as the server (see Deploy Stage A process model). No LLM\n359\t\n360\tWatches for bead close events from NEEDLE workers. Detection is CLI-only: the watcher polls `bf list --status closed` on a fixed interval (default 30s) and keeps a high-water mark (newest close timestamp already processed) to pick out new closures. On the same tick it checks each open tracked bead — every unresolved `intents.bead_ref` — via `bf show`, parsing comments past a per-bead high-water mark for `REFUSED:` entries (the circuit-breaker signal; see The Async Path). All `bf` invocations run from the aide-de-camp repo's beads workspace (see Beads-Workspace Scoping under the Escalate Strand). It never reads `.beads/` files directly — the SQLite db is the CLI's private store, and `issues.jsonl` is only a flush checkpoint that misses unflushed mutations; both are documented corruption/staleness footguns in this workspace.\n361\t\n362\tOn close:\n363\t1. Resolves the closed bead to its intent via `intents.bead_ref` and reads `session_id` from the intent row — the bead itself carries no session metadata; the mapping lives entirely in the session store\n364\t2. Looks up active surface in session store\n365\t3. Writes result to `results` table and marks the intent `resolved` (or `failed`, per The Async Path's terminal-failure rule)\n366\t4. Fires SSE push to canvas or Telegram push if no canvas active\n367\t\n368\t> ⚠ The Telegram push in step 4 is NON-FUNCTIONAL until ADR-1 lands — `src/telegram/fallback.py`'s delivery methods are stubs pointed at an unreachable bridge URL (see ADR-1). Today, a bead closing with no active canvas writes to `results` but notifies no one.\n369\t\n370\tThe same poll loop drives the async-path circuit breaker (see The Async Path): each tick it folds newly parsed refusal comments into `bead_watch` (refusal count, last reason, comment high-water mark), flags cards whose `sla_deadline` has passed, and fences stuck beads to `status=blocked`, flipping their intents to `stuck`. Breaker and SLA state live in `bead_watch` in the session store — never only in memory — so a watcher restart loses nothing.\n371\t\n372\t**Ambient monitoring tick.** The watcher daemon is also the runtime for ambient monitoring — there is no separate monitoring process. On its own timer (`tick_interval_seconds` in `config/monitoring.yaml`, default 300; the config hot-reloads per tick per the Hot-Reload Architecture), the daemon evaluates each rule in `config/monitoring.yaml` against its watched topic: `src/monitoring/ambient.py` runs the relevant fetch-matrix sources through the fetch strand, diffs the output against `topic_context_cache`, and when a rule fires writes a `results` row directly — `topic_id` set, `intent_id` NULL (system-originated, no utterance behind it), `urgency` and exception type taken from the rule via `config/monitoring.yaml` and `config/exceptions.yaml`, `summary` filled from the rule's deterministic template (no LLM anywhere on the tick). From there the result enters Surface Routing Rules like any other: SSE push to the active surface, exception-class handling when urgency is critical.\n373\t\n374\tPure I/O. Not a separately started process: FastAPI's lifespan startup spawns the watcher loop in-process, so the single Stage A run command brings up hot path, watcher, ambient monitoring, and circuit breaker together — the server cannot be up with the watcher silently absent. Liveness is verified, not assumed: the watcher stamps `last_tick_at` after every poll tick, and `GET /health` exposes a `watcher` block (`alive`, `last_tick_at`, tick count) where `alive` is true only while the task is running **and** `last_tick_at` is within 2× the poll interval. A crashed watcher task is logged and restarted with backoff by the lifespan supervisor; the `/health` gap makes the crash observable. Under Deploy Stage B the same in-process model ships inside the aide-de-camp Deployment (never a K8s Job/CronJob, per infrastructure conventions).\n375\t\n376\t---\n377\t\n378\t## Degraded-State UX\n379\t\n380\t\"Failed sources are surfaced as caveats\" is not a design. Every failure mode below maps to a **designed error card rendered from a fixed template** — these templates ship as built-in cards in the served frontend, the fourth built-in family (see Component Library → Built-in cards), filled client-side from SSE error events — so the canvas never shows a blank region, a spinner that never resolves, raw JSON, or a stack trace. These cards are what the Phase 5 known-issues register's degraded-state row points at.\n381\t\n382\t| Failure mode | Canvas presentation | Recovery action |\n383\t|--------------|---------------------|-----------------|\n384\t| One fetch source fails or times out | Normal card built from the surviving sources; a caveat strip (from `fetch_coverage`) names what's missing — e.g. \"ArgoCD unreachable — sync status omitted\" | Per-source retry on the caveat; the next dispatch on the topic refetches everything |\n385\t| ALL fetch sources fail | \"No data\" error card: intent header + per-source failure list. Fixed template, no LLM call — synthesize is skipped, there is nothing to synthesize | Retry button re-runs fetch for the intent; utterance and intent are preserved |\n386\t| ZAI proxy down / timeout / quota-exhausted at the **router** stage | Dispatch-level error card: \"Router unavailable — LLM proxy unreachable\", with the raw utterance shown so nothing is lost | Retry re-dispatches the persisted utterance. ⚠ No fallback LLM endpoint exists — a proxy outage kills the entire hot path (router **and** synthesize); a direct-API fallback is tracked as Open Question 8 |\n387\t| ZAI proxy failure at the **synthesize** stage | Degraded \"raw data\" card: the structured fetch output renders under a \"summary unavailable\" banner — fetched data is never discarded | Retry-synthesize action reuses the fetched context (no refetch) |\n388\t| SSE connection drops | Unobtrusive \"reconnecting…\" indicator; existing cards stay visible, marked stale | Client auto-reconnects (EventSource backoff + `Last-Event-ID`); on reconnect the workload-summary replay delivers anything surfaced while disconnected |\n389\t| Hot-path card still pending at 30s (server hung, dispatch lost — no SSE event will ever arrive) | Aged-pending treatment applied by the **canvas client itself**: the card exists to age at all because the canvas creates a local per-utterance pending placeholder at submit time, before any server response (split into per-thread pending cards on the dispatch ack — see Escalate Strand, Pending/ack card render path); a client-side timer from the placeholder's creation, no server dependency, shows elapsed time plus \"taking longer than expected\" (see The Async Path — Visible aging) | Retry re-dispatches the card's utterance; the aged card is replaced by the retry's result |\n390\t| Router returns malformed JSON / schema-invalid output | After one automatic corrective retry, a clarification-style card: \"Couldn't parse that into intents\", showing the utterance with an edit-and-resend action. Raw model output goes to logs, never the canvas | User edits or resends; malformed output is logged as fodder for router-prompt iteration |\n391\t\n392\tQuota exhaustion on the ZAI proxy presents identically to the proxy being down — same rows, same cards. The proxy is a single point of failure for the entire hot path; that is a deliberate Deploy Stage A trade-off (tracked as Open Question 8), and it stays visible here rather than hidden.\n393\t\n394\t---\n395\t\n396\t## Data Model\n397\t\n398\t### Session Store (SQLite)\n399\t\n400\t```sql\n401\tsessions (\n402\t  id          TEXT PRIMARY KEY,\n403\t  created_at  INTEGER,\n404\t  last_active INTEGER,\n405\t  primary_surface_id TEXT\n406\t)\n407\t\n408\tsurfaces (\n409\t  id              TEXT PRIMARY KEY,\n410\t  session_id      TEXT,\n411\t  type            TEXT,  -- 'canvas' | 'telegram' | 'audio'\n412\t  state           TEXT,  -- 'active' | 'idle' | 'disconnected'\n413\t  always_available INTEGER DEFAULT 0,  -- 1 for Telegram (⚠ aspirational: Telegram delivery non-functional; ADR-1 removes session-bound Telegram surfaces)\n414\t  last_seen       INTEGER\n415\t)\n416\t\n417\tutterances (\n418\t  id          TEXT PRIMARY KEY,\n419\t  session_id  TEXT,\n420\t  raw_text    TEXT,\n421\t  created_at  INTEGER\n422\t)\n423\t\n424\tintents (\n425\t  id           TEXT PRIMARY KEY,\n426\t  utterance_id TEXT,\n427\t  session_id   TEXT,\n428\t  topic_id     TEXT,  -- primary topic, authoritative and always set; see intent_topics\n429\t  project_slug TEXT,\n430\t  intent_type  TEXT,\n431\t  lookup_kind  TEXT,  -- lookup intents only: 'logs' | 'config' | 'docs' (router-emitted; see Intent Router). NULL otherwise\n432\t  status       TEXT,  -- 'pending' | 'dispatched' | 'resolved' | 'stuck' | 'failed' | 'cancelled'\n433\t  bead_ref     TEXT,  -- set for task-profile intents\n434\t  created_at   INTEGER,\n435\t  resolved_at  INTEGER\n436\t)\n437\t\n438\tdispatch_timings (\n439\t  intent_id                 TEXT PRIMARY KEY,\n440\t  router_ms                 INTEGER,  -- shared across intents from the same utterance\n441\t  fetch_first_source_ms     INTEGER,\n442\t  fetch_total_ms            INTEGER,\n443\t  synthesize_first_token_ms INTEGER,\n444\t  synthesize_total_ms       INTEGER,\n445\t  escalate_ms               INTEGER,  -- task-profile dispatches only; null otherwise\n446\t  sse_emit_ms               INTEGER,\n447\t  stt_ms                    INTEGER,  -- client-reported; null when unavailable\n448\t  first_render_ms           INTEGER,  -- client-reported; null when unavailable\n449\t  created_at                INTEGER\n450\t)\n451\t\n452\tresults (\n453\t  id          TEXT PRIMARY KEY,\n454\t  intent_id   TEXT,  -- NULL for monitoring-originated results (see Bead Watcher)\n455\t  result_type TEXT,  -- deterministic card-selector key, set at result-write time:\n456\t                     -- \"{intent_type}:{project_slug}\" for intent-derived results — one per\n457\t                     -- intent thread (the aggregated thread card), never per fetch source;\n458\t                     -- lookup threads insert the intent's lookup_kind:\n459\t                     -- \"lookup:{lookup_kind}:{project_slug}\" (e.g. \"lookup:logs:whisper-stt\"\n460\t                     -- vs \"lookup:config:whisper-stt\" — distinct keys, distinct cards);\n461\t                     -- \"monitoring:{project_slug}\" for monitoring-originated rows.\n462\t                     -- The hot-path component lookup keys on this column, no LLM\n463\t                     -- (see UI-Regen Agent / component_usage_patterns)\n464\t  topic_id    TEXT,\n465\t  session_id  TEXT,\n466\t  summary     TEXT,\n467\t  data        TEXT,  -- JSON\n468\t  urgency     TEXT,  -- 'critical' | 'high' | 'normal' | 'low'\n469\t  created_at  INTEGER,\n470\t  surfaced_at INTEGER,\n471\t  acked_at    INTEGER,\n472\t  previous_result_id TEXT,  -- prior result of the SAME result_type on the same topic\n473\t                            -- (diff scope; NULL when none exists) — a status result\n474\t                            -- never diffs against a brainstorm result. Pure lineage,\n475\t                            -- set across sessions (project topics are cross-session):\n476\t                            -- the diff strip renders only when this points at a result\n477\t                            -- from the current session (see Cold start & demo seed —\n478\t                            -- Topic scope vs. session scope)\n479\t  diff_summary TEXT,  -- human-readable diff summary\n480\t  diff_data    TEXT   -- JSON: detailed field diffs\n481\t)\n482\t\n483\ttopics (\n484\t  id           TEXT PRIMARY KEY,\n485\t  label        TEXT,\n486\t  type         TEXT,  -- 'project' | 'research' | 'personal' | 'exception' | 'compound'\n487\t  project_slugs TEXT,  -- JSON array\n488\t  scope        TEXT,  -- 'session' | 'cross-session' | 'global'\n489\t  session_id   TEXT,  -- null for cross-session/global\n490\t  created_at   INTEGER,\n491\t  last_active  INTEGER,\n492\t  archived_at  INTEGER\n493\t)\n494\t\n495\ttopic_context_cache (\n496\t  topic_id     TEXT PRIMARY KEY,\n497\t  context_data TEXT,  -- JSON: pre-fetched context (kubectl, git, beads results)\n498\t  fetched_at  INTEGER,\n499\t  expires_at  INTEGER\n500\t)\n501\t\n502\tfeedback_signals (\n503\t  signal_id    TEXT PRIMARY KEY,\n504\t  signal_type  TEXT,\n505\t  session_id   TEXT,\n506\t  result_id    TEXT,\n507\t  topic_id     TEXT,\n508\t  timestamp    INTEGER,\n509\t  data         TEXT,  -- JSON: signal-specific data\n510\t  surface_type TEXT,\n511\t  processed    INTEGER,  -- 0 or 1\n512\t  processed_at INTEGER\n513\t)\n514\t\n515\t-- Additional memberships for compound topics only: intents.topic_id is the\n516\t-- primary topic (always set, authoritative); this table never replaces it,\n517\t-- it only holds the extra topics a compound intent also belongs to.\n518\tintent_topics (\n519\t  intent_id TEXT,\n520\t  topic_id  TEXT,\n521\t  PRIMARY KEY (intent_id, topic_id)\n522\t)\n523\t\n524\t-- Watcher-owned: circuit-breaker and SLA state per tracked bead.\n525\t-- Persisted here (not in watcher memory) so refusal counts and SLA\n526\t-- flags survive watcher restarts. See The Async Path and Bead Watcher.\n527\tbead_watch (\n528\t  bead_ref            TEXT PRIMARY KEY,\n529\t  intent_id           TEXT,\n530\t  refusal_count       INTEGER DEFAULT 0,\n531\t  last_refusal_reason TEXT,\n532\t  last_refusal_at     INTEGER,\n533\t  comment_high_water  TEXT,     -- newest comment id/timestamp already parsed\n534\t  sla_deadline        INTEGER,  -- set at bead creation: intent-type default or registry sla_hours\n535\t  sla_flagged_at      INTEGER,\n536\t  fenced_at           INTEGER,\n537\t  created_at          INTEGER\n538\t)\n539\t```\n540\t\n541\t### Component Library (SQLite, separate DB)\n542\t\n543\t```sql\n544\tcomponents (\n545\t  id            TEXT PRIMARY KEY,\n546\t  name          TEXT,\n547\t  description   TEXT,\n548\t  html_template TEXT,\n549\t  version       INTEGER,\n550\t  created_at    INTEGER,\n551\t  last_used     INTEGER,\n552\t  usage_count   INTEGER\n553\t)\n554\t\n555\tcomponent_versions (\n556\t  component_id  TEXT,\n557\t  version       INTEGER,\n558\t  html_template TEXT,\n559\t  created_at    INTEGER,\n560\t  change_note   TEXT,\n561\t  PRIMARY KEY (component_id, version)\n562\t)\n563\t\n564\tcard_cache (\n565\t  result_id         TEXT,\n566\t  component_id      TEXT,\n567\t  component_version INTEGER,\n568\t  layout_bucket     TEXT,  -- 'compact' | 'normal' | 'expanded'\n569\t  rendered_html     TEXT,\n570\t  created_at        INTEGER,\n571\t  PRIMARY KEY (result_id, component_id, layout_bucket)\n572\t)\n573\t\n574\tcomponent_tags (\n575\t  component_id TEXT,\n576\t  tag TEXT,\n577\t  PRIMARY KEY (component_id, tag)\n578\t)\n579\t\n580\tcomponent_usage_patterns (\n581\t  component_id TEXT,\n582\t  result_type TEXT,  -- thread-level key matching results.result_type, e.g.\n583\t                     -- \"status:pbx-web\", \"lookup:logs:whisper-stt\",\n584\t                     -- \"lookup:config:whisper-stt\" (lookup keys carry lookup_kind,\n585\t                     -- so log-lookup and config-lookup components select\n586\t                     -- independently) — never per-source (\"pod-status\")\n587\t                     -- granularity; a thread's sources aggregate into one card\n588\t  match_score REAL,  -- 0-1\n589\t  sample_count INTEGER,\n590\t  last_matched INTEGER,\n591\t  PRIMARY KEY (component_id, result_type)\n592\t)\n593\t```\n594\t\n595\t**Built-in generic fallback card.** The hot-path selector (see The Hot Path / UI-Regen Agent) is a deterministic lookup: highest `match_score` in `component_usage_patterns` for the result's `result_type`, no LLM. When no component matches — a first-ever result shape, or nothing above threshold — the card does not come from this DB at all: the served frontend ships a generic fallback card (key/value grid over `result.data` plus the `summary` line, all values HTML-escaped per the render-path escaping contract — see UI-Regen Agent) as part of `src/canvas/`, and the server flags the result so the client uses it. Novel shapes therefore always render something legible even with zero library rows; the UI-regen agent later promotes recurring fallback shapes into real components. `card_cache` rows are written only for real component renders.\n596\t\n597\t**Built-in cards.** The fallback card is one of four card families that ship as fixed templates in the served frontend (`src/canvas/`) and never come from this DB: (1) the generic fallback card above, (2) the first-run welcome card (see Cold start & demo seed), (3) the pending/ack cards — the task-profile ack/pending card and the hot-path pending card with its per-source progress states — and (4) the error/clarification cards: the fixed-template cards Degraded-State UX defines (router-unavailable, all-sources-failed, degraded raw-data, malformed-router-output) plus the no-match clarification card (see Cold start & demo seed), filled client-side from SSE error events. Pending/ack cards cannot be library components even in principle: component selection keys on `results.result_type` and `card_cache` keys on `result_id`, and neither exists before the result does (task-profile results only appear at bead closure) — which is also why their lifecycle starts as a local per-utterance placeholder the canvas creates at submit time, before any server event (see Escalate Strand, Pending/ack card render path). The client fills these templates from SSE events (dispatch ack, per-source progress, bead ref, elapsed time, error details) and swaps in the component-rendered card when the result arrives. Consequence for Phase 5: built-in cards across all four families are exempt from — and never seeded for — the component-library requirements in the seeding runbook and rehearsal checklist.\n598\t\n599\t---\n600\t\n601\t## File System Layout\n602\t\n603\t```\n604\taide-de-camp/\n605\t├── adc                      ← CLI entry point (shell script or Python package)\n606\t├── README.md                ← operational/configuration reference (env vars, configuration table — the file ADR-1 cites)\n607\t├── README-PHASE4.md         ← Phase 4 verification evidence (cited by Implementation Phases)\n608\t├── config/\n609\t│   ├── registry.yaml        ← project registry (hot-reloaded by router)\n610\t│   ├── clusters.yaml        ← cluster → ArgoCD endpoint mapping (see Fetch Strand)\n611\t│   ├── monitoring.yaml      ← ambient monitoring rules\n612\t│   └── exceptions.yaml      ← exception routing rules\n613\t├── prompts/\n614\t│   ├── router.md            ← intent segmentation prompt\n615\t│   ├── synthesize.md        ← result generation prompt\n616\t│   ├── voice.md             ← voice model system prompt\n617\t│   ├── urgency.md           ← urgency classification prompt\n618\t│   ├── fetch/               ← per-intent-type fetch instructions\n619\t│   │   ├── status.md\n620\t│   │   ├── action.md\n621\t│   │   ├── lookup-logs.md   ← lookup matrices are per lookup_kind (see Intent Router)\n622\t│   │   ├── lookup-config.md\n623\t│   │   ├── lookup-docs.md\n624\t│   │   └── ...\n625\t│   └── escalate/            ← escalate/task-profile prompts\n626\t│       └── task-profile.md\n627\t├── src/\n628\t│   ├── main.py              ← FastAPI app entry point\n629\t│   ├── registry.py          ← project registry loader\n630\t│   ├── agents/              ← agent implementations\n631\t│   │   ├── self_modification.py  ← self-improvement agent\n632\t│   │   └── ui_regen.py          ← UI-regen agent\n633\t│   ├── components/          ← component library and hot-reload\n634\t│   │   ├── library.py           ← component library DB operations\n635\t│   │   └── hot_reload.py        ← hot-reload watcher\n636\t│   ├── context/             ← context warming and pre-fetch\n637\t│   │   ├── warmer.py            ← context warmer for active topics\n638\t│   │   └── prefetch.py          ← speculative pre-fetch\n639\t│   ├── conversation/        ← multi-turn conversation handling\n640\t│   ├── diff/                ← diff generation for results\n641\t│   ├── environment/         ← environment and repo discovery\n642\t│   │   └── discovery.py        ← repo scanner: seeds registry.yaml (one-time/on-demand scan); self-mod agent is sole ongoing author (see Project Registry)\n643\t│   ├── escalate/            ← escalate strand for task-profile intents\n644\t│   │   ├── handler.py           ← escalate request handler\n645\t│   │   ├── llm.py               ← LLM calls for bead formulation\n646\t│   │   └── commands.py          ├── bead creation commands\n647\t│   ├── feedback/            ← feedback processing and background analysis\n648\t│   │   ├── processor.py         ← explicit feedback processor\n649\t│   │   ├── signals.py           ← implicit feedback signal tracking\n650\t│   │   └── background_analysis.py ← background analysis bead\n651\t│   ├── fetch/               ← fetch strand (deterministic, per intent type)\n652\t│   │   ├── commands.py          ← fetch command matrix, intent types, data structures\n653\t│   │   └── orchestrator.py      ← FetchStrand: concurrent fetch execution, streaming, coverage tracking\n654\t│   ├── intent/              ← intent router (LLM classification)\n655\t│   │   └── router.py            ← intent segmentation and routing\n656\t│   ├── memory/              ← memory store and extraction (supporting store for cross-session context; not yet surfaced by any component — Future Work)\n657\t│   │   ├── store.py             ← memory persistence\n658\t│   │   └── extraction.py        ← memory extraction from results\n659\t│   ├── monitoring/          ← ambient monitoring\n660\t│   │   └── ambient.py           ← ambient monitoring rules\n661\t│   ├── realtime/            ← OpenAI Realtime API voice session\n662\t│   │   ├── session.py           ← voice session handler\n663\t│   │   ├── batching.py          ← result batching for narration\n664\t│   │   ├── continuity.py        ← audio-to-canvas session continuity\n665\t│   │   └── dispatch.py          ← tool-as-trigger dispatch\n666\t│   ├── session/             ← session store (SQLite)\n667\t│   │   └── store.py             ← session store operations\n668\t│   ├── sse/                 ← SSE broadcasting\n669\t│   │   ├── broadcaster.py       ← SSE connection registry\n670\t│   │   └── events.py            ← SSE event types\n671\t│   ├── stt/                 ← speech-to-text (whisper-stt browser fallback)\n672\t│   │   └── fallback.py          ← STT fallback client behind /api/v1/stt\n673\t│   ├── surface/             ← surface routing\n674\t│   │   └── router.py            ← result surface routing logic\n675\t│   ├── synthesize/          ← synthesize strand (LLM)\n676\t│   │   └── strand.py            ← result synthesis\n677\t│   ├── telegram/            ← Telegram fallback surface\n678\t│   │   └── fallback.py          ← Telegram delivery\n679\t│   ├── test/                ← test-harness endpoints (bypass Web Speech API for e2e tests)\n680\t│   ├── topic/               ← topic model\n681\t│   │   └── model.py             ← topic operations\n682\t│   ├── watcher/             ← bead watcher daemon\n683\t│   │   └── daemon.py            ← NEEDLE bead watcher\n684\t│   ├── canvas/              ← web frontend (SSE consumer, card renderer)\n685\t│   │   └── index.html           ← single-page canvas UI\n686\t│   └── cli/                 ← adc CLI\n687\t│       ├── main.py              ← CLI entry point\n688\t│       ├── commands.py          ← CLI commands\n689\t│       ├── config.py            ← CLI configuration\n690\t│       └── sse.py               ← SSE streaming for CLI\n691\t├── data/\n692\t│   ├── session.db           ← session store (SQLite)\n693\t│   └── components.db        ← component library (SQLite)\n694\t└── docs/\n695\t    ├── research/            ← architecture and design research\n696\t    ├── plan/\n697\t    │   └── plan.md          ← this file\n698\t    └── notes/\n699\t        ├── core-verification-evidence.md  ← phase verification evidence (cited by Implementation Phases)\n700\t        └── naming.md\n701\t```\n702\t\n703\t---\n704\t\n705\t## Deployment Model\n706\t\n707\taide-de-camp is a **live web application**, not a static site. The FastAPI server is the intelligence layer — it handles SSE connections, maintains session state, runs the router and synthesize strands, and serves the frontend HTML. There is no CDN path (no CF Pages).\n708\t\n709\t*Naming note: deployment stages are **Deploy Stage A** (bare-metal) and **Deploy Stage B** (k8s) — deliberately not \"Phase 0/1+\", which collided with the Implementation Phases numbering (deployment \"Phase 1+\" had nothing to do with implementation Phase 1). References elsewhere to the \"Phase 0 deployment\" (e.g., ADR-1) mean Deploy Stage A. Stage A is the hosting model through the screen-capture demo and public launch; Stage B is post-launch hardening.*\n710\t\n711\t### Why not static\n712\t\n713\t- SSE connections are long-lived; CDNs cannot proxy them\n714\t- WebSocket for the Realtime API voice session requires a persistent backend\n715\t- The session store (SQLite) and artifact store (prompts, registry) require a writable filesystem\n716\t- Hot-reload depends on the running server reading updated files from disk on each invocation\n717\t\n718\t### Current Deployment: Deploy Stage A (bare-metal — Hetzner server directly)\n719\t\n720\t**Status: LIVE** ✅ — running server confirmed 2026-07-20 (ADR-1 audit)\n721\t\n722\tThe server runs as a process on the Hetzner server itself, not in k8s:\n723\t- NEEDLE workers and the aide-de-camp server share the same filesystem\n724\t- Self-modification agent writes directly to `prompts/` and `config/` — hot-reload works without any coordination\n725\t- Exposed via Tailscale (the server is already on the mesh); no ingress config needed\n726\t- SQLite DBs are local files; no PVC required\n727\t\n728\tRunning command: `uvicorn src.main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000`\n729\t\n730\t**Stage A process model — exactly one process, by design.** The command above is the complete process inventory. The bead watcher (which also hosts ambient monitoring and the async-path circuit breaker — see Bead Watcher) is **not** a second command an operator must remember: FastAPI's lifespan startup spawns the watcher loop as an asyncio background task inside this same uvicorn process. A separately documented daemon is exactly the step that gets skipped, and its absence is silent — closures never surface, SLAs never fire, monitoring never ticks, while every test still passes (the \"verified-in-tests, dead live\" pattern). Startup-spawning makes that failure impossible; liveness is still checked, not assumed: `GET /health` reports `watcher.alive` / `watcher.last_tick_at` (see Bead Watcher), and the pre-demo seeding runbook verifies it in item (3).\n731\t\n732\tNo container, no CI, no ArgoCD. Managed as a long-running process (see CLAUDE.md for restart commands).\n733\t\n734\t### Release flow (Deploy Stage A)\n735\t\n736\tVersion is in `pyproject.toml` only. No CI build — runs from source.\n737\t\n738\tRelease: `bump version in pyproject.toml` → `commit` → `git tag vX.Y.Z` → `push`.\n739\t\n740\t### Future: Deploy Stage B (containerized, ardenone-cluster)\n741\t\n742\t**Status: NOT BUILT** ❌\n743\t\n744\tSession persistence and multi-surface routing are implementation Phase 1 deliverables and run on Stage A (currently PARTIAL — see Implementation Phases); they are not the trigger for this stage. The Stage B trigger is operational: uptime independent of the Hetzner box, container isolation, and standard CI/ArgoCD management. Not needed for the demo or launch. When that point arrives, containerize and move to k8s:\n745\t\n746\t```\n747\tDocker image: ronaldraygun/aide-de-camp\n748\tDeployment: ardenone-cluster, namespace: aide-de-camp\n749\tPVC: /data/ (SATA, ReadWriteOnce)\n750\t  /data/session.db        ← session store\n751\t  /data/components.db     ← component library\n752\t  /data/prompts/          ← hot-reloadable prompt files\n753\t  /data/config/           ← registry.yaml, monitoring.yaml, exceptions.yaml\n754\t```\n755\t\n756\tThe artifact store (prompts, registry) moves from the repo's working directory to the PVC. The self-modification agent updates artifacts via the aide-de-camp API (`PATCH /artifacts/{name}`), which writes to the PVC path the server reads from. The PVC artifact directory is itself a git repository: the server commits every `PATCH /artifacts/{name}` write with the same machine-generated message convention, so the \"versioning is git\" safety model — one-instruction rollback, `adc restore-artifacts` — survives the move to Stage B unchanged.\n757\t\n758\t### Future: Traefik configuration for SSE and WebSocket (Deploy Stage B)\n759\t\n760\t**Status: NOT BUILT** ❌\n761\t\n762\tTraefik's default response buffering breaks SSE. The IngressRoute needs:\n763\t\n764\t```yaml\n765\t# IngressRoute annotations\n766\ttraefik.ingress.kubernetes.io/router.middlewares: aide-de-camp-strip-prefix@kubernetescrd\n767\t\n768\t# Middleware: disable buffering, extend timeouts\n769\tapiVersion: traefik.io/v1alpha1\n770\tkind: Middleware\n771\tmetadata:\n772\t  name: aide-de-camp-streaming\n773\tspec:\n774\t  buffering:\n775\t    maxResponseBodyBytes: 0   # disable buffering\n776\t  headers:\n777\t    customResponseHeaders:\n778\t      X-Accel-Buffering: \"no\"\n779\t```\n780\t\n781\tServersTransport timeout:\n782\t\n783\t```yaml\n784\tapiVersion: traefik.io/v1alpha1\n785\tkind: ServersTransport\n786\tmetadata:\n787\t  name: aide-de-camp-longlived\n788\tspec:\n789\t  forwardingTimeouts:\n790\t    responseHeaderTimeout: 0    # no timeout on SSE streams\n791\t    dialTimeout: 5s\n792\t```\n793\t\n794\tDUCK-E's existing WebSocket IngressRoute config is the reference implementation for this cluster.\n795\t\n796\t### SQLite concurrency\n797\t\n798\t**Status: COMPLETE** ✅\n799\t\n800\tMultiple writers (bead watcher, background analysis bead, session handler) require WAL mode to avoid lock contention:\n801\t\n802\t```python\n803\t# On DB open\n804\tconn.execute(\"PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL\")\n805\tconn.execute(\"PRAGMA synchronous=NORMAL\")\n806\t```\n807\t\n808\tWAL mode allows concurrent reads during writes and serializes multiple writers without blocking. For a personal single-user app, this is sufficient.\n809\t\n810\t### adc CLI connectivity\n811\t\n812\tThe `adc` CLI is a thin HTTP client. It talks to the running server over Tailscale:\n813\t\n814\t```bash\n815\t# ~/.config/adc/config\n816\tserver_url = \"http://localhost:8000\"   # Deploy Stage A (local)\n817\t# Future: \"http://aide-de-camp.ardenone.com\" (Deploy Stage B)\n818\t```\n819\t\n820\tNo local inference. The CLI sends requests to the FastAPI backend and streams the SSE response to the terminal.\n821\t\n822\tOne exception: the kill-switch fallbacks (`adc freeze`'s `data/FREEZE` sentinel, `adc restore-artifacts`' git revert) act on the server's local filesystem, not over HTTP — they only work when `adc` runs on the server host itself. SSH in first; see the Self-Modification Agent safety model's break-glass procedure.\n823\t\n824\t### Future: CI/CD (Deploy Stage B)\n825\t\n826\t**Status: NOT BUILT** ❌\n827\t\n828\tNew Argo WorkflowTemplate `aide-de-camp-build` on iad-ci:\n829\t- Docker build → `ronaldraygun/aide-de-camp`\n830\t- Update image tag in `jedarden/declarative-config` (k8s/ardenone-cluster/aide-de-camp/)\n831\t- ArgoCD syncs automatically\n832\t\n833\tSame pattern as every other containerized service in the stack.\n834\t\n835\t---\n836\t\n837\t## Technology Stack\n838\t\n839\t| Layer | Choice | Reason |\n840\t|-------|--------|--------|\n841\t| Backend | FastAPI (Python) | Reuse DUCK-E scaffolding; SSE + WebSocket support; async-native |\n842\t| STT | Web Speech API → whisper-stt fallback | Zero-latency browser-native; whisper-stt already deployed |\n843\t| Voice session | OpenAI Realtime API (via DUCK-E session handler) | DUCK-E scaffolding already built on this |\n844\t| LLM calls | ZAI proxy (`llm-proxy.ardenone.com`) | Pay-per-token, no subscription pressure on hot path |\n845\t| Task workers | NEEDLE + Claude Code | Existing infrastructure, unchanged |\n846\t| Session store | SQLite (WAL mode) | Lightweight, local, additive-only schema; single-user app |\n847\t| Artifact store | PVC-mounted filesystem (Deploy Stage B) / local filesystem (Deploy Stage A) | Writable at runtime; hot-reload reads per-invocation |\n848\t| Frontend | Vanilla JS + Web Components | No build pipeline; SSE consumer injects server-rendered component-card HTML. Client-side rendering is limited to the built-in card templates shipped with the served frontend (`src/canvas/`): the generic fallback card, the first-run welcome card, the pending/ack card with its progress states, the error/clarification cards (see Degraded-State UX), and the aged-pending treatment on overdue cards — every component-library card arrives server-rendered |\n849\t| Deploy Stage A hosting | Hetzner server, local process | Simplest path; shared filesystem with NEEDLE workers |\n850\t| Deploy Stage B hosting | ardenone-cluster, k8s Deployment | Behind existing Traefik + Tailscale ingress |\n851\t\n852\t---\n853\t\n854\t## Implementation Phases\n855\t\n856\t**Status vocabulary.** Every phase status below carries one of two verification tiers — never read \"COMPLETE\" without its tier:\n857\t\n858\t- **verified-in-tests** — deliverables pass the automated test harness / smoke tests (the `src/test/` endpoints that bypass the Web Speech API). Says nothing about behavior on the live server for a real user.\n859\t- **verified-live** — observed working on the running server, with the date and how it was observed recorded on the status line.\n860\t\n861\tAs of 2026-07-22 no phase has reached verified-live end-to-end; the only live check on record (2026-07-20, ADR-1) disproved a Phase 1 deliverable.\n862\t\n863\t### Phase 0 — Minimal Viable Surface (~2 days)\n864\t\n865\t**Status: COMPLETE (verified-in-tests)** — not yet verified-live\n866\t\n867\t*Verification evidence:* see `docs/notes/core-verification-evidence.md` — smoke test results, 20+ runs with all tests passing (test harness, not live; date unrecorded — re-verify)\n868\t\n869\tValidates the core question: does routing + parallel dispatch reduce friction?\n870\t\n871\t- Single HTML page: textarea + mic button (Web Speech API)\n872\t- `POST /dispatch` endpoint: router → N parallel synthesize calls → SSE stream\n873\t- Results appear as basic cards as agents resolve\n874\t- No persistence, no auth, no component library, no ambient push\n875\t\n876\tDeliverable: the core query loop working end-to-end.\n877\t\n878\t### Phase 1 — Session and Topics (~1 week)\n879\t\n880\t**Status: PARTIAL** ⚠️ — session store, topics, and bead watcher are verified-in-tests; the Telegram fallback deliverable is non-functional in every deployment to date (stub methods returning `False`, unreachable hardcoded bridge URL, architectural mismatch — see ADR-1, observed live 2026-07-20). Re-opened pending ADR-1 implementation.\n881\t\n882\t*Verification evidence:* see `docs/notes/core-verification-evidence.md` — session store, SSE, surface registration (test harness, not live; date unrecorded — re-verify). Those tests passed while the Telegram stubs shipped — this is exactly why the two-tier vocabulary above exists.\n883\t\n884\tResults persist; the canvas has memory.\n885\t\n886\t- Session store (SQLite, 7+ tables with topic_context_cache and feedback_signals)\n887\t- Topic model: canvas shows one card per **(active topic, result_type)** pair, each updated in place — a status card and a brainstorm card on the same project topic coexist as distinct cards, grouped under the topic; a new result replaces only the card sharing both its topic and its result_type\n888\t- Telegram surface fallback (reuse telegram-claude-bridge)\n889\t- Bead watcher: closed NEEDLE beads push results to active surface\n890\t- Workload summary on reconnect\n891\t- Staleness indicators on cards\n892\t\n893\tDeliverable: sessions that survive browser refresh (verified-in-tests); Telegram fallback working (**not met** — see ADR-1).\n894\t\n895\t### Phase 2 — Self-Improvement Loop (~2 weeks)\n896\t\n897\t**Status: COMPLETE (verified-in-tests)** — not yet verified-live\n898\t\n899\t*Verification evidence:* see `docs/notes/core-verification-evidence.md` — component library, hot-reload manager (test harness, not live; date unrecorded — re-verify)\n900\t\n901\tThe interface can be improved by talking into it.\n902\t\n903\t- Self-modification agent (Claude Code via NEEDLE)\n904\t- Hot-reload confirmed at every artifact layer\n905\t- Component library: UI-regen agent generates first components from actual result shapes\n906\t- Canvas live-updates when a component is versioned\n907\t- Explicit feedback processed: \"always include X\" → prompt updated → hot-reloaded\n908\t\n909\tDeliverable: at least one end-to-end self-modification cycle working (user instructs change, diff surfaced, approved, takes effect without redeploy).\n910\t\n911\t### Phase 3 — Responsiveness (~2-3 weeks)\n912\t\n913\t**Status: COMPLETE (verified-in-tests)** — not yet verified-live\n914\t\n915\t*Verification evidence:* see `docs/notes/core-verification-evidence.md` — ambient monitoring, context warmer (test harness, not live; date unrecorded — re-verify)\n916\t\n917\tThe interface feels alive, not just reactive."}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"aeea5eff-b392-4e98-826c-b0b9c2715d82","uuid":"776d9429-907a-428f-af66-742dbe9653ba","timestamp":"2026-07-24T08:51:35.951Z","tool_use_result":{"type":"text","file":{"filePath":"/home/coding/aide-de-camp/docs/plan/plan.md","content":"# aide-de-camp: Application Plan\n\n## Overview\n\naide-de-camp is a universal personal interface that accepts stream-of-consciousness voice and text input, routes it to parallel agents across any domain, and renders results as a live canvas of HTML cards or as synthesized audio narration. It is built on top of existing personal infrastructure and improves itself through conversational feedback after initial bootstrap.\n\nCLI invocation: `adc`\n\n---\n\n## Problem Statement\n\nManaging multiple active projects requires constant context-switching between dedicated tabs, terminals, and chat sessions. The input channel (typing into a specific Claude Code tab) is also the routing layer, and that conflation adds cognitive overhead. The same problem extends beyond coding: research, lookups, personal context, reminders — anything that requires knowing which system to ask before you can ask it.\n\naide-de-camp eliminates that overhead by providing a single input surface that routes automatically, dispatches in parallel, and returns organized results without requiring the user to know where to ask.\n\n---\n\n## Core Architecture\n\n### The Hot Path\n\n```\nUser utterance (voice or text)\n  → STT transcription (Web Speech API or whisper-stt)\n  → Intent Router (one LLM call, haiku-class, ~500ms est.)\n      output: [{project_slug, intent_type, urgency, utterance_fragment,\n                lookup_kind (lookup intents only)}, ...]\n  → N parallel Fetch+Synthesize workers (one per intent thread)\n      Fetch: deterministic code, executes command matrix based on intent_type\n             (lookup intents: intent_type + lookup_kind)\n      Synthesize: one LLM call per thread (sonnet-class, ~1-2s est.)\n      output: {data, summary} — urgency carries through unchanged from the\n              intent thread (see Urgency ownership)\n  → Server-side card render (no LLM): deterministic component lookup via\n      component_usage_patterns, template filled into card_cache;\n      no match → result flagged for the built-in fallback card\n  → Cards streamed via SSE to active canvas — client injects the rendered\n      HTML, or renders fallback-flagged results with its built-in generic card\n```\n\nTarget: < 3 seconds from utterance to first partial result on canvas. No Claude Code session startup on this path.\n\n> The ~500ms and ~1-2s figures above — and every per-stage number in this plan — are design **estimates**. Nothing on the hot path has been measured yet. See Latency Budget & Instrumentation.\n\n### Latency Budget & Instrumentation\n\nThe <3s promise is the product's central claim, so it gets a budget, instrumentation, and a gate — not just a diagram annotation.
